Lead Feature
376 Companies, One Survey.
The spring quarter's cover-to-cover review of every issue in coverage — ranked, rated, reconciled.

This volume inaugurates the Ledger's twelfth year of continuous publication. Readers will find the full tear-sheet format preserved: one company per page, every figure shown, no summary chart pretending to be research. Read at your own pace; skim for rankings at §06.

The Quarterly Ledger
April 2026
From the Desk Of

The Editor

Chief of Research

In This Issue

We publish tear sheets on 376 companies this quarter, expanded from 342 last. Fourteen new issues enter coverage, largely in health services and specialty finance; six have been retired following M&A or insufficient float.

Distribution

Subscribers of record as of March 31. Next issue mails on July 10, 2026.

— The Editor
From the Editor

A Quiet Quarter, Loudly Priced.

Three consecutive quarters of earnings growth have arrived without the fanfare we are accustomed to associating with such episodes. The Composite advanced 4.1% on a total-return basis, dividends posted their highest aggregate payout since 2019, and two-thirds of the issues in our coverage universe finished the period with forward estimates revised upward. Yet sentiment indicators read neutral, equity-fund inflows are negligible, and the consensus narrative remains one of impending deceleration. We find this divergence instructive.

"The arithmetic of compounding has not been repealed — only overlooked. The question is not whether this market is expensive, but which corners of it remain reasonable."

Within these pages, readers will find our usual cross-section of issues: forty-two in consumer staples and discretionary; fifty-six in health services and pharmaceuticals; sixty-eight across the broad industrial and transport groups; and the remainder scattered among financials, utilities, energy, materials, and technology. Timeliness rankings have been revised on 212 issues; Safety on 94. Our five-year earnings projections for the universe have been lifted, modestly, on a trailing twelve-month basis — the first such upward revision since the third quarter of 2024.

We remind subscribers that these tear sheets are instruments of study, not recommendations. The figures are audited; the projections are ours; the decisions remain yours. Read attentively, and with patience.
